skill.name: solid-principles
skill.description: Apply SOLID principles to functional and TypeScript-first Node.js code.
skill.summary: Guides TypeScript refactoring with SOLID design principles and testable abstractions.
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external commands, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown code fences, TypeScript template literals, and substring matches in educational examples. No executable scripts, network exfiltration, credential access, prompt injection, or malicious intent were found in SKILL.md or examples.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks are Markdown TypeScript fences or TypeScript template literals in documentation. The skill does not define or run shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reported external command location is a Markdown fence delimiter or a TypeScript template literal inside documentation. No executable script file or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits occur in descriptive words or test names, not in cryptographic algorithms or hashing APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter text or Jest describe blocks. No MD5, SHA1, DES, cipher, hash, or crypto API usage appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Example Business Logic
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance hits are validation errors, database lookup examples, test identifiers, or test declarations. They do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are educational TypeScript snippets for order validation, persistence, and tests. No calls collect OS, user, network, process, or environment metadata.

content.value_statement: TypeScript services often mix validation, persistence, payments, and notifications. This skill guides SOLID refactoring with focused modules, abstractions, and testable dependencies.
actual_capabilities: Explains each SOLID principle for Node.js and TypeScript projects.
Shows violation and corrected examples for SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, and DIP.
Demonstrates dependency injection with repositories, payment gateways, and notifiers.
Uses Result-style errors and focused functions in refactoring examples.
Provides an order service refactoring from coupled code to testable modules.
Shows test-friendly dependency fakes and Jest-style verification examples.
limitations: Focused on TypeScript and Node.js examples, not every programming language.
Provides guidance and examples, not automated code transformation.
Does not verify runtime behavior or compile the shown snippets.
Assumes users can adapt examples to their project architecture.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Coupled Service
use_cases.description: Break a service into validation, persistence, payment, and notification responsibilities.

use_cases.title: Teach Practical SOLID Design
use_cases.description: Use TypeScript examples to explain each SOLID principle during mentoring or review.

use_cases.title: Improve Testability Before Changes
use_cases.description: Introduce abstractions and fakes so behavior can be tested before feature work.

prompt_templates.title: Identify SOLID Violations
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small TypeScript module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript module for SOLID principle violations. Explain each issue in simple terms and suggest one focused improvement.

prompt_templates.title: Split Responsibilities
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate refactoring of mixed business logic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this service using the Single Responsibility Principle. Separate validation, persistence, and notification concerns without changing behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Design Abstractions
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced dependency inversion for external services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply Dependency Inversion and Interface Segregation to this code. Define focused interfaces and show how implementations should be injected.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Architecture Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert migration plan for a production TypeScript service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a step-by-step SOLID refactoring plan for this service. Include risks, test strategy, module boundaries, and rollout order.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript order service validates carts, charges Stripe, writes Prisma records, and sends email in one method.
output: The review identifies Single Responsibility and Dependency Inversion violations.
The suggested design separates validation, pricing, repositories, payment, and notification.
The result is easier to test with fake dependencies and focused failure cases.

output_examples.input: A large data service interface forces read-only clients to depend on write, delete, backup, and migration methods.
output: The review flags an Interface Segregation issue.
The recommendation creates small reader, writer, and deletable abstractions.
Clients depend only on the capabilities they use.

output_examples.input: A discount function uses repeated conditional branches for every membership type.
output: The review identifies an Open/Closed Principle issue.
The recommendation moves discount behavior into replaceable strategies.
New discount types can be added without editing the main calculation flow.

best_practices: Start by naming each responsibility before extracting modules or functions.
Prefer small interfaces that match actual client needs.
Use dependency injection to make external services replaceable in tests.
anti_patterns: Do not split code only to create layers with no independent responsibility.
Do not hide concrete dependencies behind broad interfaces with unused methods.
Do not apply SOLID rules mechanically when a simple function is clearer.

faq.question: Does this skill write production-ready code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and refactoring patterns that users must adapt and verify.

faq.question: Which languages does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on TypeScript and Node.js, with functional patterns and class examples where useful.

faq.question: Can it help with tests?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show dependency injection, fakes, and Jest-style tests for refactored services.

faq.question: Does it require a specific framework?
faq.answer: No. The examples mention Prisma, Stripe, and email services, but the principles are framework independent.

faq.question: Is it useful for code reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives reviewers a structured way to discuss responsibility, extension points, substitution, interfaces, and dependencies.

faq.question: Can it replace architecture judgment?
faq.answer: No. It should support design discussion, not replace project context, runtime testing, or team standards.
